title: Ethernet Switching
description: How switches forward Ethernet frames using MAC address tables to deliver traffic only to the correct port.
fullExplanation: A switch maintains a CAM (Content Addressable Memory) table — also called MAC address table — that maps MAC addresses to switch ports. When a frame arrives, the switch learns the source MAC → port mapping. It then looks up the destination MAC: if found, it forwards only to that port (unicast); if not found, it floods to all ports except the source (unknown unicast). Broadcast and multicast frames are also flooded. This eliminates collisions and provides dedicated bandwidth per port.

forwardingModes.description: Receives entire frame, checks CRC, then forwards. Eliminates corrupt frames. Adds latency (~10µs).
Starts forwarding after reading destination MAC (first 6 bytes). Lower latency but forwards corrupt frames.
Reads first 64 bytes (collision window) before forwarding. Compromise between above two.

camTableBehavior.action: Forward only to the port mapped to destination MAC
Flood to all ports except source (until MAC learned)
Flood to all ports except source
Flood to all ports (unless IGMP snooping configured)

keyFacts: CAM table size: typically 8K-128K entries on enterprise switches
MAC aging timer: entries expire after ~300 seconds of inactivity
MAC flooding attack: overflow CAM table → switch behaves like hub → sniff all traffic
Port security: limit MAC addresses per port to prevent flooding attacks
IGMP snooping: forward multicast only to ports with interested receivers
Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) prevents Layer 2 loops in redundant topologies
Layer 3 switch: performs routing between VLANs at wire speed using ASICs
